companyCaseStudy.title: How Cloudflare Absorbed a 26M RPS DDoS: Building an Anycast Edge that Keeps the Internet Online
companyCaseStudy.summary: When Cloudflare faced a Layer‑7 DDoS peaking at ~26 million requests/second, their response was not a single firewall rule but an engineering program: scale the network, push scrubbing to the edge, and make mitigation a mix of eBPF/XDP, anycast routing and edge compute. Result: the attack was absorbed without customer downtime, and Cloudflare’s global edge architecture became intentionally overprovisioned and highly automated.
companyCaseStudy.explanation:
## Hook
Cloudflare once mitigated an HTTP DDoS that peaked at roughly 26 million requests per second. That’s not a traffic spike you paper over with a bigger origin server — it’s an existential test of your network, routing, and filtration. Their answer was simple in concept and complex in execution: move the fight to the edge.

## Problem
A major customer-facing site was hit by a high‑volume, low‑amplification Layer‑7 attack that looked like legitimate HTTP GETs. With the internet increasingly hostile and traffic volumes surging globally, Cloudflare needed to ensure: 1) no single scrubbing center became a bottleneck, 2) legitimate traffic was served with low latency, and 3) customers had per-site custom mitigation without manual intervention.

Numbers at stake
- Peak attack: ~26,000,000 requests/sec (publicly reported by Cloudflare)
- Global edge: 200+ cities / ~275+ PoPs (Cloudflare’s distributed footprint)
- Target: zero customer downtime, minimal added latency

## Challenge
- Layer‑7 attacks are syntactically valid HTTP requests — hard to drop without false positives.
- Centralized scrubbing would require massive east‑west bandwidth and add latency.
- Need per‑customer rules (WAF, rate limits, JS challenges) but executed close to where traffic lands.
- Telemetry and control must be real‑time so mitigation can adjust as the attack morphs.

## Solution — push intelligence and capacity to the edge
Cloudflare’s approach combined three pillars: massive anycast capacity, programmable edge filtering, and rapid telemetry/automation.

1) Overprovisioned Anycast Edge
- Each PoP announces customer IPs via BGP anycast so attack traffic is distributed across the entire footprint.
- The network is intentionally overprovisioned (backbone capacity >> average load) so spikes are absorbed without saturation.

2) Layered, local mitigation stack at every PoP
- L1 (network): use BGP anycast + network ACLs to route and reject obvious noise early.
- L2 (socket/kernel): XDP / eBPF filters drop volumetric garbage close to NIC.
- L3 (HTTP edge): nginx/fast HTTP proxy and rate limiters (token buckets per IP/AS), WAF rules, JS challenges and CAPTCHA.
- L4 (edge compute): Cloudflare Workers allow per-customer custom logic (e.g., custom heuristics) executed in the same PoP.

3) Automation & Telemetry
- Real-time metrics aggregated in per-PoP pipelines allow global coordination: when one PoP sees abnormal signatures, mitigation rules propagate automatically.
- Control plane pushes WAF/rate-limit rule updates to the fleet in seconds.

### How these pieces fit (architecture)
```mermaid
graph LR
  Client[Client / Botnet] --> Internet
  Internet --> Anycast[Anycast BGP]
  Anycast --> PoP1[PoP - Edge Node]
  Anycast --> PoP2[PoP - Edge Node]
  PoP1 --> XDP1[XDP/eBPF drop]
  XDP1 --> HTTP1[HTTP Edge: Rate limit / WAF / JS Challenge]
  HTTP1 --> Cache[Edge Cache]
  HTTP1 --> Worker[Cloudflare Worker (custom rules)]
  Cache --> Origin[Customer Origin]
  HTTP1 --> Logs[Telemetry Pipeline]
  Logs --> ControlPlane[Mitigation Control Plane]
  ControlPlane --> PoP2
```

## Concrete technical decisions
- Anycast first: distribute the attack across hundreds of PoPs instead of concentrating on a few scrubbing centers.
- Edge first: implement cheap, fast drops (XDP/eBPF) before moving to slower, more CPU‑heavy HTTP checks.
- Stateless token buckets per IP/7‑tuple at the edge to prevent single clients from exhausting HTTP workers.
- Use Workers and edge configuration for per‑site custom mitigation; this avoids hitting origin for decisions.
- Instrument everything: per‑PoP and per‑route telemetry to automatically escalate mitigation when signatures change.

## Before / After (comparison)
| Metric | Before (centralized scrubbing) | After (edge-first anycast scrubbing) |
|--------|--------------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| Peak attack absorbed | Limited by scrub center capacity (tens of Gbps) | ~26M RPS absorbed across global edge |
| Latency impact on legitimate users | Large (extra hop to scrubbing center) | Minimal (most checks at edge, single hop) |
| Time to deploy mitigations | Minutes to hours (manual) | Seconds (automated rules pushed to PoPs) |
| False positive surface | Higher (coarse scrubbing) | Lower (per-site logic via Workers)

## Results
- The attack was absorbed without customer downtime.
- Because filtering happened at the PoP, backbone and origin bandwidth were preserved.
- The deployment validated key investments: more PoPs, larger backbone, eBPF/XDP investment, and Workers for per-customer customization.

## Why this worked (short answer)
Anycast + local, layered filtering turns a single huge problem into hundreds of manageable smaller problems. Overprovision the edge, drop cheap bad traffic early, and push custom logic to the same place where traffic lands.

## Practical analogies
Think of a flood: rather than diverting all water to a single reservoir (central scrubbing), you build many distributed levees (PoPs) and open local sluice gates (XDP/eBPF filters) — the flood dissipates over the entire landscape.

## Risks and tradeoffs
- Cost: overprovisioning and many PoPs is expensive; Cloudflare amortizes by multi-tenant usage.
- Complexity: distributed mitigation requires robust telemetry and orchestration to avoid inconsistent rule sets.
- Edge compute correctness: pushing logic to Workers makes mitigations fast but you must ensure deterministic behavior across PoPs.

## Quick checklist if you want to borrow this approach
- Deploy PoPs/points of presence as close to user base as possible.
- Implement cheap kernel/NIC level filters (e.g., XDP/eBPF) to drop garbage early.
- Use token buckets and rate limits per source (IP/AS) at the edge.
- Provide per-customer edge logic with a safe sandbox (Workers, Lua, etc.).
- Build a real-time telemetry & rule distribution control plane.
keyTakeaways: Push mitigation to the edge: distribute attack load via anycast so no single scrubbing center becomes a bottleneck (Cloudflare absorbed a ~26M RPS Layer‑7 attack).
Drop early, compute later: use XDP/eBPF for cheap packet drops, then apply more expensive HTTP/WAF checks only to surviving traffic.
Automate rules and telemetry: real‑time metrics and an automated control plane let you adapt mitigations in seconds instead of hours.

concept.title: Stop the Race: Practical Distributed Locks — Patterns, Pitfalls, and Safe Recipes
concept.summary: Distributed locks let multiple processes coordinate access to a shared resource across machines. They solve races such as duplicate job runs, inventory oversells, or concurrent schema changes — but implemented incorrectly they create outages or subtle consistency bugs.
concept.explanation:
## Why it matters

Imagine two workers both see "process invoice #123" and both charge the customer. Or two schedulers both run the same daily job. On a single machine you use an OS mutex or file lock — across many machines you need a distributed lock: a way to ensure *only one* process at a time can hold the right to perform an operation.

**Analogy:** Think of a single key to a storeroom. Whoever holds the key may access the stock. If the key is duplicated, or the lock times out too soon, you get inconsistency. If the lock server is a tiny hut and it burns down, the whole system may be blocked.

---

## What a distributed lock gives you (and what it doesn't)

- **Mutual exclusion:** only one client holds the lock at a time. *Safety.*
- **Bounded hold time (TTL):** prevents indefinite blocking if a client dies. *Liveness.*
- **Ownership:** a lock should be releasable only by its owner (token-based) to avoid accidental releases.

But: distributed locks are *not* a magic bullet for correctness. Many problems can be solved safer with idempotency, optimistic concurrency (CAS), or designing away the need for a lock.

---

## High-level flow (sequence diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  participant ClientA
  participant LockService
  participant ClientB
  ClientA->>LockService: tryAcquire(resource, clientToken, ttl)
  LockService-->>ClientA: granted
  ClientB->>LockService: tryAcquire(resource, clientToken2, ttl)
  LockService-->>ClientB: denied
  ClientA->>LockService: extend(resource, clientToken)
  LockService-->>ClientA: extended
  ClientA->>LockService: release(resource, clientToken)
  LockService-->>ClientA: released
  ClientB->>LockService: tryAcquire(resource, clientToken2, ttl)
  LockService-->>ClientB: granted
```

---

## Implementations & tradeoffs

| Approach | Pros | Cons | When to use |
|---|---:|---|---|
| Redis SET NX + EX (single instance) | Simple, fast | Single point of failure; owner safety needs token check | Small clusters or non-critical locks where SLAs are forgiving |
| Redlock (multi Redis masters) | Higher fault tolerance across nodes | Complex; debated correctness in partitioned networks | When you need Redis-style locks across failures and accept tradeoffs |
| ZooKeeper ephemeral znodes | Strong ordering, watches, good for leader election | Operates as a cluster; more operational overhead | Leader election, fine-grained coordination, concurrency primitives |
| Postgres advisory locks | Durable, transactional-friendly | DB load increases; limited to DB cluster | DB-bound workflows, transactional coordination |

---

## BEFORE / AFTER (concrete example: scheduled job)

| Before (no lock) | After (with distributed lock) |
|---|---|
| Two schedulers may run the same job concurrently — duplicate side effects | Only the scheduler that acquires the lock runs the job; others skip or wait |
| Hard-to-reproduce race failures in production | Deterministic single-run behavior (subject to proper TTL/renewal) |

---

## Code recipes (practical, safe patterns)

### Minimal Redis lock (concept)

Use SET key value NX PX ttl. Store a unique token (UUID). Only delete if token matches.

```python
# python + redis-py (conceptual)
import uuid
from redis import Redis
r = Redis()
key = 'lock:job:cleanup'
token = str(uuid.uuid4())
# Acquire
ok = r.set(key, token, nx=True, px=10_000)  # 10s TTL
if ok:
    try:
        do_work()
    finally:
        # Safe release using Lua to check token and delete atomically
        r.eval("""
            if redis.call('get', KEYS[1]) == ARGV[1] then
                return redis.call('del', KEYS[1])
            else
                return 0
            end
        """, 1, key, token)
else:
    skip_work()
```

Notes: always use a unique token and atomic check-and-del to avoid deleting another owner's lock.

### Postgres advisory lock

```sql
-- Try to acquire without blocking
SELECT pg_try_advisory_lock(12345);
-- Release
SELECT pg_advisory_unlock(12345);
```

In application code, use pg_try_advisory_lock to make a non-blocking check. Because the lock is tied to the session, it is released if the session ends — a nice safety.

---

## Common pitfalls and how to avoid them

- *TTL too short:* long operations can outlive TTL, causing another client to acquire the lock while the first is still running. Avoid by choosing a conservative TTL and/or renewing the lock periodically (only by the owner).
- *Incorrect release:* deleting a lock without verifying ownership can release someone else’s lock. Always use an owner token and atomic compare-and-delete (Lua in Redis).
- *Clock skew:* don't rely on synchronized clocks for correctness. Use TTL-based life rather than client wall-clock checks.
- *Single point of failure:* a single Redis or DB node can be a bottleneck; use cluster-aware solutions if you need higher availability.
- *Blocking long-running work inside a lock:* keep critical sections small. If processing is long, consider breaking it into a short locked phase + asynchronous work with idempotency.
- *Thinking locks solve every race:* many workflows are better handled with idempotent operations, optimistic concurrency (version checks), or leader election.

---

## When to use distributed locks

Good for: resource-level exclusivity (migrations, leader election, job deduplication), coordinating external systems where you can't do strong DB transactions.

Avoid for: very high-throughput short operations (locks add latency and contention), or where better alternatives exist (idempotency, compare-and-swap, partitioning work by key).

---

## Patterns for robustness

- Generate a **unique owner token** at acquisition time, and verify it when releasing.
- Keep **lock-critical sections short**; delegate heavy work to background jobs that are idempotent.
- Use **renewal only if owner** and implement a safe refresh protocol (extend TTL before expiry). If renewal fails, abort work.
- Prefer **server-side atomic operations** (Lua in Redis, DB transactions, ZooKeeper ephemeral nodes) over multi-step client logic.

---

## Small checklist before adding a distributed lock

1. Can idempotency or optimistic concurrency solve it instead? If yes — prefer that.
2. Do you have a clear owner token and atomic release? If not — don’t deploy.
3. Is your TTL large enough and do you handle renewals safely?  
4. Monitor lock wait times and failures; alert on long waits.
keyTakeaways: Distributed locks enforce mutual exclusion across processes, but are tricky — always use owner tokens, atomic release, and conservative TTLs.
Prefer simpler alternatives (idempotency, optimistic concurrency) when possible; use locks for coordination, not as a first resort.
Pick the right tool: Redis for speed, ZooKeeper for strong coordination semantics, DB advisory locks when the DB is central; avoid single-node SPOFs for critical locks.
